SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper provides experimental evidence for its main claims (e.g. intrinsic evaluation of 50 sampled lines per model, Table 1 reporting that the constrained character model had 70% lines with zero mistakes, and an extrinsic evaluation where machine poems were judged human 54% of the time). However, methodological details are incomplete or lacking: small sample sizes (only 50 lines), manual stress annotation without inter-annotator agreement, and no reported statistical significance tests. The authors acknowledge transliteration-induced errors (Table 2, CMU dictionary/HMM transliteration), which undermines some claims but is not quantitatively resolved, leaving gaps between claims and fully supported evidence.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper proposes two implementations (a phonetic-level neural LM and a constrained character-level LM using a weighted finite state machine) which combine known components (LSTMs, CMU pronunciation lexicon, HMM/ n-gram transliteration, WFST constraints). Prior work is acknowledged (e.g. Zhang and Lapata 2014) and many techniques are standard in NLP, so the contribution is an application-driven combination rather than a wholly new algorithmic idea. The framing of learning rhythm via phonetic encoding and constraining content with an FST is a non-trivial engineering design but reads as incremental rather than highly novel.

CLARITY.justification: The manuscript is poorly organized and contains many obvious repetitions and formatting issues (multiple duplicated sentences such as the 'Neural language models ... have garnered significant research interest' and repeated bibliographic fragments), which make sections hard to follow. While the main contributions are stated, notation and experimental setup lack clarity (datasets, training sizes, participant details for extrinsic eval are not clearly specified) and figures/tables referenced (Table 1/2) are described but not shown in the provided text.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The work includes both intrinsic and extrinsic evaluations and examines transliteration accuracy, which shows some depth. However, important analyses are missing: no ablation or sensitivity studies (e.g. impact of transliteration vs. model capacity), limited experiment scale (50-line intrinsic samples), few implementation details for reproducibility (dataset sizes, model hyperparameters beyond BPTT=150 timesteps), and only partial discussion of limitations (transliteration errors noted but not fully analyzed).

IMPACT.justification: Automatic generation of rhythmic verse is an interesting niche problem in NLP and the reported human-evaluation outcome (machine poems judged human 54% of the time) suggests practical relevance. Nevertheless, the contribution is application-focused and incremental in methods; while it may spur follow-up work on form-aware generation, it is unlikely to substantially change broader NLP/ML research agendas on its own.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The topic (form-aware poetry generation using neural LMs and WFST constraints) sits squarely within NLP/ML conference scope and will interest the venue's audience. The paper uses standard models and evaluations expected in the field (LSTMs, pronunciation lexicons, human evaluations), though some experimental and reporting standards could be improved to meet the highest-tier expectations.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares the phonetic model, a constrained character-level model, and an unconstrained character baseline, and cites related work (e.g. Zhang and Lapata 2014). However, comparisons appear limited: there is no discussion of more or stronger baselines, no clear matching of experimental conditions across methods, and no statistical comparisons reported. The most relevant competing approaches are cited but not comprehensively benchmarked against.
